original_abstract: Iron (Fe) is fundamental to life on earth. In the human body, it is both essential and harmful if above threshold. A similar balance applies to other elements: calcium (Ca), magnesium (Mg), and trace elements including copper (Cu), zinc (Zn), lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), mercury (Hg), and nickel (Ni). These elements share some proteins involved in the absorption and transport of Fe. Cu and Cd can inhibit Fe absorption, while excess of Fe may antagonize Cu metabolism and reduce ceruloplasmin (Cp). Excessive Fe can hinder Zn absorption and transferrin (Trf) can bind to both Zn and Ni. Ca is able to inhibit the divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1) in a dose-dependent manner to reduce Fe absorption and low Mg concentra- tions can exacerbate Fe deficiency. Pb competitively inhibits Fe distribution and elevated Cd absorption reduces Fe uptake. Exposure to Hg is associated with higher ferritin concentrations and Ni alters intracellular Fe metabolism. Fe removal by phlebotomy in hemochromatosis patients has shown to increase the levels of Cd and Pb and alter the concentrations of trace elements in some types of anemia. Yet, the effects of chronic exposure of most trace elements remain poorly understood.
humanized_abstract: Iron (Fe) is vital to life on Earth. Homeostasis in the human body is necessary to prevent both iron deficiency and iron overload. Other essential and toxic elements require a similar balance for optimal health and function, like calcium (Ca), magnesium (Mg) and trace elements such as copper (Cu), zinc (Zn), lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), mercury (Hg) and nickel (Ni). Several proteins are common to iron and these elements. Thus, while Cu and Cd inhibit iron absorption, iron excess can alter Cu metabolism and decrease ceruloplasmin (Cp). Moreover, iron excess can also negatively affect zinc (Zn) absorption. Transferrin, a protein that binds iron, is also able to bind Zn and Ni. Similarly, Ca inhibits DMT1 (divalent metal transporter 1) iron absorption in a dose-dependent manner, whereas a low Mg concentration is associated with iron deficiency. Pb affects iron distribution, and an increased Cd absorption also decreases iron uptake. Finally, Hg exposure is associated with increased ferritin concentrations, whereas Ni is involved in the intracellular regulation of iron homeostasis. The effect of chronic exposure to the levels of most trace elements is still poorly understood as iron depletion by phlebotomy in patients with hemochromatosis has been shown to lead to increases in Cd and Pb levels and changes in the levels of other trace elements in iron deficient or iron deficient anemic states.
